title: Mixed-media journal page
subtitle: Building a layered journal page with gesso ground, watercolour wash, washi tape collage, and stamp marks
excerpt: A mixed-media journal page is built in layers from opaque ground to transparent glaze, with different media occupying different visual depths. Gesso provides the ground and unifies disparate elements; watercolour adds transparent colour; washi tape and torn paper add texture and pattern; stamps add a final graphic mark. Each layer responds to and partially obscures the one beneath it.
sourceNotes: General mixed-media and art journalling tradition. No single canonical source; the layered approach is documented widely in art journalling literature from the 2000s onwards.

glossaryTerms.definition: A layer of white acrylic gesso brushed or palette-knifed onto the page to create an opaque, slightly textured surface. The gesso ground unifies underlying collage elements and provides a consistent absorbency for the next media layer. It dries quickly to a matte, lightly toothy finish.
Any cut or torn paper piece, washi strip, or found-paper fragment adhered to the page surface. Collage elements add texture and pattern before the gesso layer, or between subsequent media layers.

A diluted watercolour wash applied over a dry opaque layer. The glaze tints the surface while allowing the texture and marks underneath to show through. The opaque-to-transparent sequence is the core logic of layered media work.

content.text: A mixed-media page builds bottom to top: textured base, opaque ground,

, surface marks. The sequence matters: each layer modifies rather than replaces what is beneath. Gesso applied over a collage element does not fully hide its texture or edges; those print through the gesso as part of the surface.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm (loose sheet or journal page)

content.text: and apply them to the page with a thin smear of PVA. Use torn edges rather than cut edges, torn edges blend more naturally under the gesso layer. Washi tape strips laid diagonally, strips of text from printed paper, and fragments of wrapping paper all work. Cover 50-70% of the page surface; leave some areas clear.
Once the collage is dry, apply a coat of

with a palette knife or stiff brush, working in one direction across the page. Do not aim for a perfectly even coat: some variation in thickness and some visible brush or knife marks add to the texture. Allow to dry completely (15-20 minutes).

Apply a diluted watercolour wash over the dry gesso. One or two colours work better than many, a single warm colour across most of the page with one contrasting accent is a reliable starting point. Work quickly with a large brush and let the wash find its own edges rather than painting to a planned boundary.

Once the watercolour is dry, add surface marks: stamp impressions using the craft stamp, ruled lines with the ruling pen, or gestural brush marks. Surface marks at this final layer sit on top of everything else and define the finished character of the page.

items.symptom: The page warps severely on drying
items.cause: Too much water in the watercolour layer on light cartridge paper.
items.fix: Use 130 gsm as the minimum weight. For heavily washed pages, work on the page while it is in the open journal so the binding provides some resistance to warping, or tape the page edges down to a flat board before wetting.

items.symptom: The collage elements show as hard, raised edges through the gesso that look unintentional
items.cause: The collage elements were too thick or had folded edges.
items.fix: Flatten all collage elements under a weight for 10 minutes after gluing. Use thinner materials, tissue paper, lightweight washi, and very thin text-weight printed papers show less edge shadow through gesso than card weight.
